trajectory.description: Observation: eval still fails because predict.py/model.py try to load the base model from an absolute /data path, which the isolated eval container validates as an invalid HF repo path. Local inspection also shows the zero-shot model emits XML attributes like <point x1="..." y1="...">, but the parser misreads these as bbox numbers, causing 0 score. Analysis: we need a self-contained checkpoint that includes the processor/tokenizer/config plus a reliable relative symlink/copy to the offline base model weights, and a parser that correctly handles attribute-style point tags. Since first priority is a working eval pipeline, not yet finetuning, fix offline resolution and parsing, test predict locally from the checkpoint path only, then resubmit eval immediately. Decision: update train.py to create a self-contained checkpoint with a symlinked/copy base_model subdir and meta.json, patch model.py resolution and parser, run local val scoring on a small subset to verify nonzero behavior, then submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: local wrapper checkpoint now loads, but eval failed because the packaged `base_model` inside checkpoint is a symlink that does not survive into the isolated eval workspace; zero-shot score is also 0.0. Analysis: first priority is to make the checkpoint self-contained for eval by copying metadata and actual weight index files into the checkpoint (or the whole model dir), not symlinking. Given the zero-shot baseline is ineffective, we should immediately pivot to a stronger but still simple trained baseline that does not require huge VLM finetuning. A practical route within budget is a lightweight vision-language regressor built on frozen image/text encoders from the local Qwen processor? But no easy text encoder offline. Simpler: use a small CNN over downsampled screenshots plus instruction bag-of-words/char embeddings to regress a point; this may beat 0 and trains quickly on 7k rows. However task domain is heterogeneous; better is to use a language-conditioned cross-attention model over image patches trained from scratch/lightweight. Decision: fix checkpoint packaging now so eval works, and in the same iteration replace the zero-shot inference with a fast supervised model: MobileNetV3 image encoder + text embedding GRU + fusion MLP predicting normalized point and bbox, trained on train split for a few epochs and validated on val. Also keep robust predict.py output and submit eval with the trained checkpoint.

Analysis: the current predict.py still uses the old zero-shot API (generate_raw/parse_prediction/load_model_and_processor) while iteration 6 switched training to a direct supervised regressor saved as checkpoints/simple_model.pt. So eval is likely producing invalid outputs because the prediction path is incompatible with the checkpoint. Before spending more training time, we must align model.py and predict.py with the trained checkpoint and verify end-to-end on val. Decision: rewrite model.py and predict.py around the simple supervised model only, make predict.py emit valid clipped point+bbox predictions from the .pt checkpoint, and run a quick local val prediction/score to confirm non-missing outputs.

trajectory.description: Observation: predict/load contract is now aligned enough to produce valid outputs, but local val grounding_score is only 0.0032 (1/312), so the simple global pooled regressor is badly underfitting GUI grounding. Analysis: a single pooled vector cannot localize fine UI elements. We need a spatial model that outputs a heatmap conditioned on text, while keeping training fast. A lightweight FiLM-conditioned fully convolutional network over a 320x320 image can predict a click point heatmap and derive bbox around the point; this should substantially improve point accuracy with modest compute. Decision: replace the current pooled regressor with a spatial heatmap model using frozen-small text encoder (embedding+GRU), train for ~8 epochs, then run local val prediction/score and submit eval immediately if nontrivial.

trajectory.description: Observation: the new heatmap model crashed immediately with a target/prediction spatial-size mismatch: target heatmap is 40x40 (1600) while model output is 20x20 (400). Analysis: the backbone downsamples 320->160->80->40->20, so either the target must be 20 or the model must upsample. Keeping a 40x40 target is preferable for localization resolution; simplest fix is to add a lightweight upsampling head from 20x20 to 40x40 and also correct the augmentation bug that flips twice in training. Then run a short training/validation cycle and local scoring before submitting eval. Decision: patch model.py/train.py accordingly, train for a few epochs, generate val predictions, and score them end-to-end.

Analysis: this is no longer a contract bug; the model is fundamentally not learning transferable GUI grounding. With 19 iterations and ample time left, the highest-upside move is to switch to the recommended stronger baseline: LoRA fine-tuning of the offline Qwen2.5-VL-3B model on a manageable subset, because the task is instruction-conditioned localization and the current from-scratch CNN lacks UI semantics. We should first implement a proper parser/utilities plus a minimal SFT training pipeline and run a small subset/1-epoch experiment to measure feasibility and get a real val score before scaling. Decision: add a Qwen LoRA training path, robust coordinate parser/decoder, and a matching predict.py that uses the fine-tuned VLM checkpoint; then run a short subset training and local val scoring. trajectory.description: Observation: Qwen LoRA path is the right direction, but the first training step OOMed almost immediately at batch_size=1 with current pixel budget and default attention/memory settings. Analysis: for Qwen2.5-VL-3B, the biggest levers are lower max_pixels, gradient checkpointing, disabling cache, narrower LoRA target set, and shorter subset/steps. We need a fast feasibility run that completes and gives a local val score before any eval submission. Decision: patch training/inference utilities to use much smaller image token budgets, memory-saving model settings, and consistent metric logging; then run a tiny 1-epoch subset LoRA training and local val prediction/scoring end-to-end.

Observation: the tiny LoRA run completed under memory constraints, but val grounding_score is still 0.0 with many missing/invalid outputs, meaning the issue is now mostly output-format learning/parsing rather than pipeline breakage. Analysis: training on concatenated prompt+target without masking prompt tokens makes the model waste capacity copying the prompt; additionally target format using bbox when many labels are points may hurt. The fastest high-upside fix is to (1) mask labels so loss is only on assistant target tokens, (2) train only point outputs by converting every sample to a canonical <point>x y</point> target using target_point if available else bbox center, which aligns with the metric and is easier to learn, and (3) improve parser to catch more generated variants. Keep subset modest to fit time, then validate locally and submit eval if nonzero. Decision: patch model/train for point-only SFT with prompt masking, add stronger parsing, run a 1-epoch subset training and local val scoring end-to-end, and submit eval if results improve.
